messages.content:
Almost all of the values in the iron studies of an
IDA patient are expected to be below. Which of
the following parameters will be increased?
a. Tsat
b. TIBC
c. Ferritin
d. Serum iron
d. Serum iron

A 52 year old male came in with dyspnea on
several steps. On physical examination, he was
tachycardic, bp of 140/80, with neck vein
distention, a loudS3 gallop, bibasal rales and a
bipedal edema. Upon workup, his fasting blood
glucose revealed 200 mg/dl with HbA1c of 10,
creatinine of 2.0 mg/dl What is the ideal management of blood glucose for this kind of
patient?
a. Start insulin therapy short acting and
monitor the patients sugar every hour,
while give an additional metformin and
another oral hypoglycemic like
pioglitazone to optimally control blood
glucose
b. Combine Metformin and DPP4 inhibitors
before initiating insulin therapy. Then
monitorHbA1C after a month to gauge
the appropriate timing of insulin therapy.
c. Metformin and an additional oral
hypoglycemic will be sufficient, insulin
therapy may be added later.
d. Initiate insulin intermediate acting to be
administered twice daily, while an
additionalGLP 1 agonist can be added.
Monitoring of blood glucose should be
done pre breakfast and 2 Hour post
lunch and dinner

d. Initiate insulin intermediate acting to be administered twice daily, while an additional GLP 1 agonist can be added. Monitoring of blood glucose should be done pre breakfast and 2 Hour post lunch and dinner. 

The patient described is likely suffering from heart failure and renal dysfunction. According to American Diabetic Association guidelines, insulin therapy is recommended for patients with HbA1c >10%, especially those with symptoms of hyperglycemia or recent diagnosis of type 2 diabetes. Metformin is contraindicated in patients with renal dysfunction. Therefore, a combination of intermediate-acting insulin and GLP-1 agonist is an appropriate management option. Patients with heart failure may benefit from GLP-1 agonists as they do not cause fluid retention. Blood glucose should be monitored pre breakfast and 2-hour post-lunch and dinner to evaluate the effectiveness of therapy.

One of the following statements is true:
a. The essential components to
hemodialysis are dialyzer and blood
delivery system (3, includes the
dialysate)
b. AV fistula, AV graft, permanent catheter
and IJ catheter are considered as
permanent access to dialysis.
c. The major cause of death in patients
with ESRD is cardiovascular disease.
d. The leading cause of ESRD is
hypertension.

c. The major cause of death in patients with ESRD is cardiovascular disease. 

While all of the statements may be partially true, only option c is entirely correct. Option a is missing the mention of the dialysate, which is an essential component for hemodialysis. Option b is correct in identifying the various types of permanent access to dialysis, but omits the mention of temporary access options like femoral catheter, jugular catheter, etc. Option d is not entirely correct, as the leading cause of ESRD is diabetes in developed countries, followed by hypertension.

M.M, 55 years old diabetic for 20 years had
cellulitis of both lower legs. Shenotes that she
seldom goes to the restroom to urinate from 10
times per day previously. She had good blood
pressure and her sugars are within 100-200
mg/dl. True of sepsis-associated AKI:
a. Bone marrow or renal biopsy can be
diagnostic
b. Characteristic courses are rise in serum
creatinine within 1-2 days, peak 3-5
days and recovery within 7 days of
antibiotic.
c. Decrease in GFR with sepsis can occur
even in the absence of overt
hypotension 
d. FeNa is usually < 1%

c. Decrease in GFR with sepsis can occur even in the absence of overt hypotension.

Sepsis-associated AKI can occur even without overt hypotension, as it can lead to renal hypoperfusion and ischemia. Options a, b, and d are not true regarding sepsis-associated AKI. Bone marrow or renal biopsy is not the first diagnostic option for sepsis-associated AKI. The characteristic course of sepsis-associated AKI can vary, and recovery can take longer than seven days even with appropriate antibiotic therapy. FeNa can be useful in differentiating prerenal AKI from intrarenal AKI, but is not a defining feature of sepsis-associated AKI.

The following statements are TRUE regarding
the management of hyperglycemic crises,
except
a. Hypomagnesemia may develop during
DKA therapy and may also require
supplementation
b. The major non metabolic complication of
DKA therapy is cerebral edema
c. Long-acting insulin in combination with
subcutaneous short-acting insulin
should be administered as soon as the
patient resumes eating
d. Monitoring serum ketones daily is
mandatory to check for resolution of
DKA

b. The major non-metabolic complication of DKA therapy is cerebral edema.

While cerebral edema is a serious but rare complication of DKA, it is not the major non-metabolic complication associated with DKA therapy. The other options are true regarding the management of hyperglycemic crises. Hypomagnesemia may develop during DKA therapy and may require supplementation. Long-acting insulin in combination with subcutaneous short-acting insulin should be administered as soon as the patient resumes eating. Monitoring serum ketones daily is necessary to check for the resolution of DKA.

The following statements are TRUE regarding the management of hyperglycemic crises, except
a. Hypomagnesemia may develop during DKA therapy and may also require supplementation
b. The major non metabolic complication of DKA therapy is cerebral edema
c. Long-acting insulin in combination with subcutaneous short-acting insulin should be administered as soon as the patient resumes eating
d. Monitoring serum ketones daily is mandatory to check for resolution of DKA

b. The major non metabolic complication of DKA therapy is cerebral edema.

While cerebral edema is a serious but rare complication of DKA, it is not the major non-metabolic complication associated with DKA therapy. The other options are true regarding the management of hyperglycemic crises. Hypomagnesemia may develop during DKA therapy and may require supplementation. Long-acting insulin in combination with subcutaneous short-acting insulin should be administered as soon as the patient resumes eating. Monitoring serum ketones daily is necessary to check for the resolution of DKA.

A 27 yo female who is pregnant, was diagnosed
to have antiphospholipid syndrome. Which of the
following should be prioritized for preventive
therapy?
a. hepatic dysfunction
b. thrombosis
c. arthritis
d. renal damage

b. Thrombosis 

Antiphospholipid syndrome is associated with an increased risk of thrombosis and its complications, including pulmonary embolism, deep vein thrombosis, and stroke. In a pregnant patient with antiphospholipid syndrome, preventing thrombosis is a significant concern, as it can lead to adverse pregnancy outcomes such as miscarriage, stillbirth, and preeclampsia. Preventive therapy for this patient should, therefore, prioritize the prevention of thrombosis. Options a, c, and d may occur in patients with antiphospholipid syndrome, but prevention of thrombosis is usually of greater concern due to its potentially life-threatening consequences.

A 29-year-old female presents to your clinic
complaining of painful swelling of both hands.
She also notes being very stiff in the morning
and it takes much longer to get ready for work
than previously. On P.E., it revealed
redness,swelling, and tenderness of the
proximal interphalangeal joints and
metacarpophalangeal joints. Laboratory results
revealed positive rheumatoid factor and negative ANA. Which of the following medications is the
most likely to improve her joint pain symptoms?
a. D-penicillamine
b. methotrexate
c. gold
d. NSAID

b. Methotrexate

The patient described is likely suffering from rheumatoid arthritis, as evidenced by her symptoms, physical examination, and laboratory results. Methotrexate is a Disease-Modifying Anti-Rheumatic Drug (DMARD) and the first-line treatment for rheumatoid arthritis. It can improve joint pain symptoms and slow the progression of joint damage. D-penicillamine, gold, and NSAIDs are also used in the treatment of rheumatoid arthritis, but methotrexate is the most effective and most commonly used.

Which among the following would you
recommend for the detection,prevention, or
management of diabetes-related complications
a. quarterly diabetes-related eye exam
b. biannual diabetes-related neuropathy
exam
c. daily px examination of the foot
d. monthly diabetes-related kidney exam

a. Quarterly diabetes-related eye exam

Diabetes can lead to various complications affecting different organs like the eyes, nerves, feet, and kidneys. Regular monitoring and management can help in the early detection and prevention of these complications. A quarterly diabetes-related eye exam is recommended to detect and prevent diabetic retinopathy, a condition that can lead to blindness if left untreated. Biannual diabetes-related neuropathy exam and daily foot examination can help in the early detection and prevention of diabetic neuropathy and foot ulcers that can result in amputation. Monthly diabetes-related kidney exam is not necessary, but regular monitoring of kidney function through laboratory tests is necessary to detect kidney damage in the early stages.

An 18-year-old female presents to your clinic for
evaluation of symptoms of weight loss, tremor,
and heat intolerance. Her past medical history is
negative and her only medication is the oral contraceptive pill. On P.E., vital signs showedBP
96/64 mmhg, 110 bpm, and the thyroid scan
reveals an enlarged non tender gland without
nodules. The heart and lungs are normal but the
skin feels warm to touch, and she has a fine
tremor of her hands. Laboratory revealed a low
TSHand elevated free T4 and T3 levels. Which
of the following cardiac findings is most likely to
occur in her?
a. pericardial effusion
b. atrial fibrillation
c. sinus tachycardia
d. bradycardia

c. Sinus tachycardia 

The patient described is likely suffering from hyperthyroidism, as evidenced by her symptoms, physical examination, and laboratory results. Sinus tachycardia is a common cardiac finding in patients with hyperthyroidism due to the increased metabolic demand. It is characterized by a heart rate exceeding 100 beats per minute, and it is associated with symptoms such as palpitations, chest pain, and shortness of breath. Pericardial effusion can occur in patients with hyperthyroidism, but it is a less common finding. Atrial fibrillation and bradycardia are not typically associated with hyperthyroidism, except in severe cases.

What is the screening Pap cytology test
recommendation in a 22 year old asymptomatic
female not known to be at increased risk for
cervical cancer?
a. screen every 5 years
b. screen every 3 years
c. screen every year
d. screen every 2 years

b. Screen every 3 years.

According to the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists (ACOG) and the US Preventive Services Task Force (USPSTF), asymptomatic females should begin cervical cancer screening at age 21 years. For women aged 21 to 29 years, screening with cytology (Pap smear) alone every 3 years is recommended. For women aged 30 to 65 years, screening with either cotesting (cytology and HPV tests) every 5 years or cytology alone every 3 years is recommended. For women over 65 years of age, cessation of screening may be appropriate if they have had regular screening with negative results in the past.

Which statement is not true of the nutritional
carbohydrate recommendations for adults with
prediabetes?
a. sucrose preferred over fructose
b. minimize sucrose containing foods
c. consider using glycemic index
d. estimate grams of carbohydrate in diet

a. Sucrose preferred over fructose. 

The statement "sucrose preferred over fructose" is not true of the nutritional carbohydrate recommendations for adults with prediabetes. Sucrose and fructose are both simple carbohydrates that can contribute to an increased risk of developing diabetes and other health problems when consumed in excess. Therefore, minimizing the intake of added sugars, including sucrose and fructose, is recommended. Other options, such as considering the glycemic index and estimating the grams of carbohydrates in the diet, are valid recommendations for individuals with prediabetes.

According to the ACR criteria for classification
for RA, how many points would you need to fulfill
the requirement for definite RA?
a. ≥4
b. ≥7
c. ≥5
d. ≥6

d. ≥6

According to the American College of Rheumatology (ACR) criteria for classification of rheumatoid arthritis (RA), you would need to fulfill the requirement of at least 6 points for definite RA. The criteria are:

1. Morning stiffness in and around the joints lasting at least one hour before maximal improvement (1 point)
2. Swelling of three or more joint areas (0-5 points depending on the number of joints involved)
3. Swelling of the proximal interphalangeal, metacarpophalangeal, or wrist joints (1 point for each involved joint)
4. Symmetrical joint swelling (0-3 points depending on the number of joints involved)
5. Rheumatoid nodules (1 point)
6. Positive serum rheumatoid factor (1 point)
7. Radio-graphic changes (0-5 points depending on the degree of changes)

Therefore, a minimum score of 6 is required for definite classification as RA.

The most common symptom of iron deficiency
anemia
a. Dizziness
b. Palpitations
c. Inability to concentrate
d. Fatigue

d. Fatigue

The most common symptom of iron deficiency anemia is fatigue. Other common symptoms include weakness, pallor, shortness of breath, dizziness, headache, palpitations, and decreased exercise tolerance. Inability to concentrate can also occur due to fatigue and other symptoms of anemia. However, fatigue is considered the most common symptom as it occurs in almost all individuals with iron deficiency anemia.

Which of the following is the criteria to diagnose
antiphospholipid syndrome?
a. Recurrent pregnancy lost + arterial
thrombosis + venous thrombosis +
positive APSantibody
b. Recurrent pregnancy lost with/without
arterial or venous thrombosis + positive
APSantibody
c. Either recurrent pregnancy lost or
arterial or venous thrombosis + positive
APSantibody
d. Recurrent pregnancy lost + arterial or
venous thrombosis

b. Recurrent pregnancy loss with/without arterial or venous thrombosis + positive APS antibody

The criteria to diagnose antiphospholipid syndrome include recurrent pregnancy loss with/without arterial or venous thrombosis, and the presence of positive antiphospholipid antibodies (anticardiolipin antibody, lupus anticoagulant, and/or anti-beta-2-glycoprotein-I antibody) on two separate occasions at least 12 weeks apart. The combination of pregnancy loss and thrombosis is not required, as either condition alone can be sufficient to diagnose antiphospholipid syndrome. However, the presence of positive antiphospholipid antibodies is necessary for the diagnosis.

This sonographic pattern is highly suggestive of
thyroid malignancy
a. spongiform
b. microcalcifications
c. Multiple internal cystic structures
d. All of the above

b. Microcalcifications. 

Among the listed sonographic patterns, microcalcifications are highly suggestive of thyroid malignancy. Microcalcifications appear as small, bright, echogenic foci within a thyroid nodule on ultrasound, and they are commonly observed in malignant nodules. Spongiform appearance and multiple internal cystic structures alone are not specific for thyroid malignancy and can be seen in both benign and malignant nodules. However, when combined with other suspicious features, such as microcalcifications, they can increase the likelihood of thyroid malignancy.

This sonographic pattern is highly suggestive of thyroid malignancy
a. spongiform
b. microcalcifications
c. Multiple internal cystic structures
d. All of the above

b. Microcalcifications. 

Among the listed sonographic patterns, microcalcifications are highly suggestive of thyroid malignancy. Microcalcifications appear as small, bright, echogenic foci within a thyroid nodule on ultrasound, and they are commonly observed in malignant nodules. Spongiform appearance and multiple internal cystic structures alone are not specific for thyroid malignancy and can be seen in both benign and malignant nodules. However, when combined with other suspicious features, such as microcalcifications, they can increase the likelihood of thyroid malignancy.

A 19-year-old girl develops sudden-onset
nonbloody diarrhea. She was previously well
and is not taking any medications or travelled
anywhere recently. Her abdomen is soft and
nontender on examination and the anion gap is
normal. What is the most likely acid base
disorder
a. respiratory alkalosis
b. respiratory acidosis
c. metabolic acidosis
d. metabolic alkalosis

c. Metabolic acidosis. 

The sudden onset of nonbloody diarrhea can result in metabolic acidosis due to the loss of bicarbonate through the stool. The anion gap is normal in metabolic acidosis, suggesting that there are no significant amounts of unmeasured anions or cations in the blood. Respiratory alkalosis and acidosis are caused by changes in the partial pressure of carbon dioxide (PaCO2) and are not likely
